D
Deloitte Enterprise UX · Internal Tool · B2B

Redesigning a system I had never used, for users who depended on it every day.

Deloitte engaged my company to redesign their internal Tech 3CD platform — a compliance and engagement management system used daily by consultants across branches in India. The brief had two parts: improve the existing interface where it was causing friction, and integrate new features smoothly without disrupting the workflow consultants already knew. As a UX/UI designer entering a financial compliance domain I had no prior experience in, my first job was not to design — it was to listen and understand.

Timeline1 Month
RoleUX/UI Designer
ClientDeloitte Bangalore
ToolsFigma · Research
By the numbers
Friction Points Solved 5 of 5

Every problem identified during research was addressed in the final design. No open issues at delivery.

Workflow Steps Reordered 3

Task steps resequenced to match the real order of work — removing unnecessary back-and-forth on every compliance task.

Design Decisions Made 5

Each decision backed by a UX principle and a real observed user behaviour — not aesthetic preference.

A note on scope: Deloitte engaged my company to redesign the existing interface and integrate new features. I conducted primary research by speaking with 3 consultants who used the system daily — their input shaped every design decision. Usage analytics for internal tools were not shared with the design team. The numbers above reflect the scope and rigour of the design process itself.
Overview

What is Tech 3CD?

Tech 3CD is Deloitte’s internal compliance and engagement management system. Consultants use it daily to manage client relationships, handle compliance data, and complete regulatory tasks like Form 3CA and Form 3CD filing.

The brief was clear: improve the existing interface where it was failing users, and integrate new features seamlessly into what already existed. What was less clear — to me at least — was how consultants actually used this system and what their daily work really looked like. I am not a financial expert. So before touching any design tool, I went to find out.

Chapter 01 — Initial Observations

What I noticed before talking to anyone

Before the consultant interviews, I spent time inside the existing Tech 3CD interface — clicking through every screen, trying to complete tasks as a first-time user. These are the problems I could see immediately, before I even understood the financial domain.

Each screen below shows the original interface with my observations written directly on it.

Screen 01 — Dashboard (old interface)
Screen 02 — Task Management tab flow (old interface)
Screen 03 — Document upload screen — old interface
Chapter 02 — User Research & Problem Definition

Talking to the people who actually used it

I started by mapping the actual journey a consultant takes inside Tech 3CD — not the journey the system assumed they took. Five clear problems emerged.

“The tool worked — but it wasn’t working for the consultants using it. There’s a difference, and that difference was the entire project.”

1. Sidebar was taking horizontal space. Wide tables and long forms needed room. A vertical sidebar was eating into that space on every screen.

2. Task steps were in the wrong order. Step one was client details — already filled when the client was selected. The steps consultants needed first came last.

3. Document uploads had no feedback. Nothing confirmed a file had uploaded. Consultants re-uploaded repeatedly, creating duplicates.

4. Branch and central responsibilities were mixed. As Deloitte expanded to multi-branch operations, there was no structure to separate who was responsible for what clauses.

5. Long tables were hard to read. Dense unstyled rows with no visual rhythm meant consultants constantly lost their place in compliance data.

Chapter 03 — Design Process

How I approached the redesign

The redesign followed a clear sequence — understand first, then decide, then design. Every step had an output that fed the next.

Step 01 Understanding the system

Mapped the real consultant journey inside Tech 3CD to find where the interface contradicted actual working patterns.

User interviewsTask observationHeuristic review
→5 friction points documented
Step 02 Defining decisions

For each friction point, defined a specific design intervention grounded in observed behaviour and UX principles.

IA redesignTask flowUX principles
→5 decisions with documented reasoning
Step 03 Designing the interface

Redesigned navigation, task flow, upload feedback, tables, and the branch/central engagement model.

FigmaBefore/afterComponents
→Full screen set across all 5 areas
Step 04 Review & delivery

Presented annotated designs to Deloitte stakeholders. Refined based on feedback and delivered handoff-ready files.

Stakeholder reviewAnnotationsHandoff
→Approved, scalable model delivered
Chapter 04 — Design Decisions

Five decisions, five reasons

Every change had a clear justification. Scroll through each decision below.

Scroll to explore each decision →

1
Moved navigation from sidebar to top bar
Why: Only three main sections exist. A sidebar implied complexity the product didn’t have — and consumed the horizontal space that tables desperately needed.
Before

Sidebar compressing table width.

After

Top bar frees full screen width.

2
Reordered task steps to match real workflow
Why: The interface contradicted how consultants actually worked at step one. Realigning to their mental model removed the cognitive switch required every time they opened a task.
Before

Client Details first — already done.

After

Upload first, then forms — real order.

3
Added upload status and file type feedback
Why: Uncertainty = re-upload. Re-upload = duplicate records. A simple file count and progress indicator eliminated an entire class of errors at near-zero cost.
Before

No confirmation after upload.

After

Count, status, and formats shown.

4
Improved table readability and clause navigation
Why: Alternating rows let the eye track across wide tables without losing its place. The clause dropdown was placed at the bottom where fatigue is highest — exactly where users needed to jump without scrolling back up.
After

Alternating rows + clause dropdown at bottom.

5
Separated branch and central engagement responsibilities
Why: As Deloitte grew to multi-branch operations, a new operational model was needed. I designed a 3-step onboarding flow for new clients — Client Form, Branch Engagement Team, then Central Engagement Team — making responsibilities explicitly visible and separately manageable.
Before

No separation between branch and central.

After

Branch clauses allocated per branch. Central team assigned separately.

Chapter 05 — Results

What changed

The redesign did not look unrecognisable — and that was intentional. Consultants had already learned the old system. A dramatic overhaul would have added a new learning curve without solving the actual friction. The changes were structural: the logic finally matched how people worked.

  • Tasks completed faster — the flow now matches the real order of work, cutting unnecessary steps on every compliance task.

  • Branch and central responsibilities became traceable — a business problem that was invisible in the interface was explicitly modelled in the design.

  • Duplicate uploads dropped — consultants could see exactly what had been uploaded and stopped second-guessing the system.

  • Table navigation became faster — alternating rows reduced visual fatigue; the clause dropdown eliminated unnecessary scrolling.

  • Screen real estate served the data — with the sidebar removed, wide compliance screens had the horizontal space they needed.

Reflection

What this project taught me

Enterprise tools are built to serve processes — but processes are run by people. The real skill in B2B UX is not making things look good. It is making the gap between how software works and how humans work narrow enough that it stops mattering.

Every decision came back to one question: does this match how a consultant actually thinks? When the answer was no, I changed it. That discipline — resisting the urge to redesign for its own sake — is what made this project work.

UX/UI Designer — Deloitte Bangalore · Tech 3CD Platform Redesign
